content.text: are two popular variants of the Naive Bayes classifier designed for
, particularly text classification — they differ in how they represent features (word counts vs. binary presence) and what distribution they assume for the data.

attrs.alt: Multinomial & Bernoulli Naive Bayes — concept overview

content.text:
where word repetition is significant
- Data where features are

Document: "I love this movie movie"

Multinomial (word counts):
Word    Count
I       1
love    1
this    1
movie   2  ← repetition counted!

Simplified version:
$$P(x_i|c) = \frac{\text{Count of word } i \text{ in class } c}{\text{Total words in class } c}$$

Features: Word frequencies in documents
Document A: "free money free offer" → free: 2, money: 1, offer: 1
Document B: "hello world how are" → hello: 1, world: 1, how: 1, are: 1

Multinomial NB learns:
P("free"|spam) = count(free in spam) / total words in spam
: A Naive Bayes variant specifically for

where presence/absence matters more than frequency
- Data where features indicate
Short texts (SMS, tweets), only presence matters

Document: "I love this movie movie"

Bernoulli (binary presence):
Word    Present?
I       1
love    1
this    1
movie   1  ← repetition IGNORED!

Where:
$$P(x_i=1|c) = \frac{\text{Count of documents with word } i \text{ in class } c}{\text{Total documents in class } c}$$

Scenario: Spam detection

SMS (short): "FREE money NOW!!!"
→ Bernoulli: free=1, money=1, now=1
→ Works well for short text

Email (long): "free free free offer offer money money money"
→ Multinomial: free=3, offer=2, money=3
→ Better accuracy with repetition
: The difference is the underlying distribution — you select the probability rule based on your data type.

from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB, BernoulliNB
from sklearn.feature_extraction import CountVectorizer, HashingVectorizer

# ===== Multinomial NB (word counts) =====
# Step 1: Convert text to word counts
vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
X_counts = vectorizer.fit_transform(texts)  # Frequency matrix

# Step 2: Train Multinomial NB
model_mnb = MultinomialNB(alpha=1.0)  # alpha = smoothing parameter
model_mnb.fit(X_counts, labels)

# ===== Bernoulli NB (binary presence) =====
# Step 1: Convert text to binary presence
vectorizer_bin = HashingVectorizer(binary=True)  # Binary matrix
X_binary = vectorizer_bin.fit_transform(texts)

# Step 2: Train Bernoulli NB
model_bnb = BernoulliNB(alpha=1.0)
model_bnb.fit(X_binary, labels)
: Threshold for converting features to binary (for Bernoulli)

Text classification is one of the most common machine learning tasks in industry: spam filtering, sentiment analysis, topic routing, language detection, content moderation, and customer intent recognition all require fast, reliable classification of text at scale. Naive Bayes classifiers — particularly the Multinomial and Bernoulli variants — provide a practical baseline that is fast to train, interpretable, and surprisingly competitive with far more complex models on many real-world text datasets.

The two variants address a concrete engineering question: should a word counted ten times in a document carry more weight than a word that appears once? Multinomial NB says yes — it accumulates frequency evidence. Bernoulli NB says no — it only cares whether the vocabulary item appeared at all. Choosing the wrong variant for your data type (e.g., applying Bernoulli to long documents where frequency matters) leads to measurably lower accuracy that is hard to diagnose without understanding the underlying distributions.

At an organisational level, both models are interpretable enough to audit. A regulator or product manager can ask 'why was this email marked as spam?' and receive a direct answer in terms of high-weight vocabulary — something that is impossible with a black-box neural classifier. This interpretability advantage keeps Naive Bayes in production at companies that operate under compliance or explainability requirements.
Both models reduce to counting word occurrences per class — a single pass over the dataset. Training a Multinomial NB on 100 000 emails takes milliseconds on a laptop.
The model stores only a probability per (word, class) pair. A 50 000-vocabulary model with 2 classes needs just 100 000 floats, far less than any neural approach.
Because the independence assumption simplifies the estimation problem, both models generalise well from small training sets where deep learning models would overfit.

Bag-of-words features are sparse (most words absent in any document). NB exploits sparsity by only accumulating likelihoods for present features (Multinomial) or using Bernoulli terms for all features.
New training examples can be incorporated by updating counts without retraining from scratch — useful for streaming or online classification.
The highest log-likelihood words per class directly explain predictions, supporting audit and debugging.
Conditional independence assumption is almost always violated:

'Naive' means the model assumes each word is independent of every other given the class. In practice, words co-occur (e.g., 'New' and 'York'). This misspecification limits maximum accuracy on tasks with strong word co-occurrence structure.
Very common words (stop words) accumulate large counts even after TF-IDF weighting is unavailable. Careful preprocessing (stop word removal, TF-IDF transformation) is required.
For a large vocabulary with many irrelevant words, the product of absence probabilities can dominate and suppress genuine signal. This hurts performance on large vocabularies.
Without Laplace smoothing (alpha > 0), any test word unseen in training produces a zero probability and collapses the entire product to zero.

The independence assumption causes posterior probabilities to be pushed toward 0 or 1 far more than the true Bayesian posterior. Calibration correction (Platt scaling, isotonic regression) is needed before using these probabilities for decision thresholds.
Not suitable for continuous or ordinal features:
Both variants require discrete (count or binary) inputs. Gaussian NB exists for continuous features, but neither Multinomial nor Bernoulli NB should be used directly on float-valued data.
How It Works — Mathematics in Depth
Both models apply Bayes' theorem to compute the posterior probability of class c given document x:
The denominator P(x) is constant across classes and is ignored for classification — we only need the argmax over classes. For numerical stability, all multiplications are converted to log-sums.
For document x with word counts x_1, x_2, ..., x_|V| (vocabulary size |V|), the class-conditional log-likelihood is:
where the smoothed word probability for class c is:

Here alpha is the Laplace smoothing parameter (default 1.0). Setting alpha=0 gives maximum likelihood estimation with zero-frequency problems; alpha=1 adds one pseudo-count per word per class; larger alpha increasingly smooths toward a uniform distribution.
For binary feature vector x (x_i in {0, 1}), the class-conditional log-likelihood is:

attrs.latex: \log P(x \mid c) = \sum_{i=1}^{|V|} \left[ x_i \cdot \log \hat{P}(w_i \mid c) + (1 - x_i) \cdot \log (1 - \hat{P}(w_i \mid c)) \right]

content.text: The second term is the crucial difference from Multinomial NB: Bernoulli NB explicitly penalises absent words. If a word is strongly associated with class c but absent from the document, the (1 - P) factor reduces the posterior for c. Multinomial NB would simply skip that word. This makes Bernoulli NB sensitive to vocabulary size and document length in ways that Multinomial NB is not.

Use Multinomial NB when: your features are word counts or TF-IDF values (continuous TF-IDF requires care but works in practice), documents are relatively long so repetition carries signal, you need a fast interpretable baseline before committing to a deep learning model, or you are working with count data outside of text (e.g., categorical variable frequency encoding).

Use Bernoulli NB when: your features are binary (word present/absent, user clicked/did not click, sensor triggered/not triggered), documents are short (tweets, SMS, short queries), you want explicit penalisation of absent features, or you are prototyping with a bag-of-words and want the simplest possible model.

Avoid both when: features are continuous and not easily discretised, the independence assumption is severely violated and calibrated probabilities are required for downstream decision-making, or accuracy on the task clearly exceeds the ceiling of linear classifiers (in which case SVMs with TF-IDF or fine-tuned BERT-class models are more appropriate).

from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB, BernoulliNB
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer, TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix
import numpy as np

# ── Sample dataset: 8 emails (spam vs. ham) ──────────────────────────────────
emails = [
    "free money now click here",        "win cash prize offer",
    "limited time offer free gift",     "get free pills online now",
    "meeting at 3pm on Tuesday",        "project deadline is Friday",
    "can you review my pull request",   "lunch tomorrow works for me",
]
labels = ["spam","spam","spam","spam","ham","ham","ham","ham"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    emails, labels, test_size=0.25, random_state=42, stratify=labels
)

# ── Multinomial NB: word counts ──────────────────────────────────────────────
mnb_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("vec", CountVectorizer(min_df=1, stop_words=None)),
    ("clf", MultinomialNB(alpha=1.0)),
])
mnb_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)

# ── Bernoulli NB: binary presence ────────────────────────────────────────────
bnb_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("vec", CountVectorizer(binary=True)),   # converts counts to 0/1
    ("clf", BernoulliNB(alpha=1.0)),
])
bnb_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)

# ── Evaluate both ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
for name, model in [("Multinomial NB", mnb_pipeline), ("Bernoulli NB", bnb_pipeline)]:
    preds = model.predict(X_test)
    print(f"\n=== {name} ===")
    print(classification_report(y_test, preds, zero_division=0))

# ── Inspect feature log-probabilities for interpretability ────────────────────
vec = mnb_pipeline.named_steps["vec"]
clf = mnb_pipeline.named_steps["clf"]
feature_names = vec.get_feature_names_out()
classes = clf.classes_

print("\nTop 5 words per class (Multinomial NB):")
for i, cls in enumerate(classes):
    top_idx = np.argsort(clf.feature_log_prob_[i])[-5:][::-1]
    print(f"  {cls}: {[feature_names[j] for j in top_idx]}")

# ── Cross-validation comparison ───────────────────────────────────────────────
print("\n5-fold CV accuracy (Multinomial NB):",
      cross_val_score(mnb_pipeline, emails, labels, cv=5).mean().round(3))
print("5-fold CV accuracy (Bernoulli NB):",
      cross_val_score(bnb_pipeline, emails, labels, cv=5).mean().round(3))
Real-World Example: News Article Topic Classification

A news aggregator wants to route incoming articles to one of four desks: Technology, Sports, Politics, Finance. Multinomial NB is chosen because articles are long (500–2000 words), word frequency provides strong signal ('blockchain' appearing 10 times is more informative than once), and the team needs an auditable model to explain routing decisions to editors.

from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
import json

# Simulated article training data (abbreviated)
training_articles = [
    {"text": "The Federal Reserve raised interest rates by 25 basis points citing persistent inflation. Bond yields rose sharply as investors priced in further tightening. Bank stocks declined on margin compression fears.", "topic": "Finance"},
    {"text": "Scientists at MIT unveiled a new transformer architecture that reduces attention complexity from O(n^2) to O(n log n) using sparse key routing.", "topic": "Technology"},
    {"text": "The national team qualified for the World Cup finals after defeating rivals 3-1 in a tense playoff. The striker scored twice in the second half.", "topic": "Sports"},
    {"text": "Parliament passed the emergency budget by a slim majority amid opposition walkouts. The finance minister defended the spending increases as necessary.", "topic": "Politics"},
    # ... hundreds more in practice
]

articles = [a["text"] for a in training_articles]
topics   = [a["topic"] for a in training_articles]

# TF-IDF + Multinomial NB pipeline
pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("tfidf", TfidfVectorizer(
        max_features=20_000,
        ngram_range=(1, 2),    # unigrams + bigrams for phrase signal
        sublinear_tf=True,     # apply log(1 + tf) to dampen high counts
        min_df=2,
    )),
    ("clf", MultinomialNB(alpha=0.1)),  # lower alpha for large vocabulary
])

pipeline.fit(articles, topics)

# Route an incoming article
new_article = "The central bank announced quantitative tightening measures to combat inflation, sending the dollar higher against major currencies."
prediction = pipeline.predict([new_article])[0]
probabilities = dict(zip(pipeline.classes_, pipeline.predict_proba([new_article])[0]))

print(f"Routed to desk: {prediction}")
print("Confidence breakdown:")
for desk, prob in sorted(probabilities.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1]):
    bar = '#' * int(prob * 40)
    print(f"  {desk:12}: {bar} {prob:.3f}")
Mistake 1: Applying Multinomial NB to TF-IDF with Negative Values

# WRONG: TfidfVectorizer with use_idf=True can produce values fine for MNB,
# but raw TF-IDF values from some configurations are NOT non-negative.
# MultinomialNB requires non-negative features and will raise ValueError
# if the input contains negative values.

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
import numpy as np

texts = ["hello world", "foo bar baz"]
labels = [0, 1]

# This works because TfidfVectorizer default output is always >= 0:
vec = TfidfVectorizer()
X = vec.fit_transform(texts)
print("Min value:", X.min())  # 0.0 — safe

# WRONG: manually normalised or centered features break MNB:
X_centered = X - X.mean()  # now contains negatives
try:
    MultinomialNB().fit(X_centered, labels)
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")
    # Negative values in data passed to MultinomialNB (input X)

# CORRECT: Use StandardScaler + ComplementNB for TF-IDF, or
# just use raw CountVectorizer output with MultinomialNB:
from sklearn.naive_bayes import ComplementNB
cnb = ComplementNB()          # handles TF-IDF and sparse float inputs
cnb.fit(X, labels)
print("ComplementNB works with TF-IDF:", cnb.predict(X))
Mistake 2: Forgetting to Set binary=True for Bernoulli NB

# WRONG: Using CountVectorizer without binary=True with BernoulliNB.
# BernoulliNB expects binary features. CountVectorizer returns counts (0, 1, 2, ...).
# sklearn's BernoulliNB has a 'binarize' parameter (default 0.0) that handles this,
# but relying on that default silently computes the WRONG thing if you intend
# to experiment with different binarise thresholds.

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.naive_bayes import BernoulliNB

texts = ["spam spam spam free money", "meeting at 3pm"]
labels = ["spam", "ham"]

# Implicit binarisation inside BernoulliNB (works but easy to misconfigure):
vec = CountVectorizer()
X_counts = vec.fit_transform(texts)
# BernoulliNB will binarise X_counts internally using binarize=0.0
# (any count > 0 becomes 1). This is correct but NOT transparent.

# CORRECT: be explicit about binary representation:
vec_binary = CountVectorizer(binary=True)  # enforces 0/1 at vectorization step
X_binary = vec_binary.fit_transform(texts)
# Now X_binary is already 0/1; BernoulliNB receives what it expects.
bnb = BernoulliNB(binarize=None)  # don't binarise again inside the model
bnb.fit(X_binary, labels)
print("Predictions:", bnb.predict(X_binary))

Lowercase, strip punctuation, remove stop words, and optionally apply stemming or lemmatisation before vectorisation. Raw text with casing and punctuation inflates vocabulary size with redundant tokens.
The smoothing parameter alpha has a large effect on accuracy. Search over [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0] using GridSearchCV rather than relying on the default 1.0.
Use TF-IDF with Multinomial NB for better accuracy:
TfidfVectorizer with sublinear_tf=True often outperforms raw CountVectorizer because it dampens the influence of common words without requiring explicit stop word lists.
Consider ComplementNB as a better Multinomial:
ComplementNB (a Multinomial variant designed for imbalanced datasets) consistently outperforms MultinomialNB on text classification benchmarks by using complement class statistics.
Calibrate probabilities if using them for thresholds:
Apply CalibratedClassifierCV(method='isotonic') if you need reliable posterior probabilities for custom decision thresholds rather than just the argmax class.
Always wrap vectoriser + classifier in an sklearn Pipeline to prevent data leakage during cross-validation and to enable clean GridSearchCV parameter naming (e.g., clf__alpha).

Both Multinomial and Bernoulli NB are among the fastest text classifiers available. Training complexity is O(N * V) where N is the number of training documents and V is vocabulary size — a single pass over the data to accumulate counts. This is dominated by the vectorisation step, not the Naive Bayes fitting step.

Prediction complexity is O(C * V) per document where C is the number of classes — a dot product of the log-probability vector for each class with the document vector. For large vocabularies (V = 100 000), this is still sub-millisecond per document, making Naive Bayes suitable for real-time scoring pipelines without batching.

The scalability advantage comes into its own in online or streaming settings. sklearn's partial_fit API allows Multinomial and Bernoulli NB to be updated with new batches of documents without retraining. This is particularly useful for email spam filters that need to adapt to evolving spam vocabulary without periodic full retrains.

# sklearn: the standard implementation
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB, BernoulliNB, ComplementNB
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer, TfidfVectorizer, HashingVectorizer
from sklearn.calibration import CalibratedClassifierCV
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Example: full production pipeline with hyperparameter search
pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("vec", TfidfVectorizer(sublinear_tf=True)),
    ("clf", MultinomialNB()),
])
param_grid = {
    "vec__max_features": [10_000, 50_000, None],
    "vec__ngram_range":  [(1, 1), (1, 2)],
    "clf__alpha":        [0.01, 0.1, 1.0],
}
search = GridSearchCV(pipeline, param_grid, cv=5, scoring="f1_macro", n_jobs=-1)
# search.fit(X_train, y_train)
# print("Best params:", search.best_params_)

# NLTK: includes a Naive Bayes implementation with feature dict inputs
# Useful when features are not a flat count vector
import nltk
# nltk.download('movie_reviews')
from nltk.classify import NaiveBayesClassifier
# classifier = NaiveBayesClassifier.train(train_featuresets)
# classifier.show_most_informative_features(20)  # built-in interpretability

# spark.ml: distributed NB for datasets that don't fit in memory
# from pyspark.ml.classification import NaiveBayes as SparkNB
# from pyspark.ml.feature import CountVectorizer as SparkCV

# For very large vocabularies: HashingVectorizer avoids storing vocabulary
hashing_pipe = Pipeline([
    ("vec", HashingVectorizer(n_features=2**18, alternate_sign=False, norm='l2')),
    ("clf", MultinomialNB()),
])
# hashing_pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)  # no vocabulary stored; memory O(1) in features

Naive Bayes classifiers are vulnerable to adversarial input manipulation because their decision boundaries are linear in the log-probability space. A spammer who knows your word-frequency thresholds can add innocuous high-frequency ham words to a spam email to push the posterior below the classification threshold — a technique called 'word stuffing'. Mitigation requires regularly retraining on fresh data and using ensemble methods that are harder to reverse-engineer.

A reliability concern specific to Multinomial NB is vocabulary drift. A model trained on a corpus from 2020 may under-classify documents containing vocabulary that emerged later (e.g., new brand names, slang, technical terms) because those words have zero counts in training and are either ignored or treated as unknown tokens. Monitoring the proportion of out-of-vocabulary tokens in production traffic is a useful early-warning signal for model staleness.

For Bernoulli NB, reliability degrades when vocabulary size grows significantly between training and serving. If the production vocabulary is much larger than training, the model accumulates large absence penalties for words it was never trained to recognise, systematically pushing posteriors toward classes with fewer high-weight words. Periodic vocabulary update with partial_fit on new data mitigates this problem.
Multinomial NB models word frequency (counts) and is best for long documents where repetition carries signal.
Bernoulli NB models binary presence/absence and is best for short texts or when only occurrence matters, not frequency.
Both models reduce classification to computing a weighted sum of log-probabilities — fast, sparse, and interpretable.
Laplace smoothing (alpha > 0) is mandatory to handle zero-frequency words; tune alpha via cross-validation.
ComplementNB is a drop-in replacement for MultinomialNB that typically outperforms it on imbalanced datasets.
Calibrate class probabilities with CalibratedClassifierCV if you need reliable posteriors for custom thresholds.
Neither model is appropriate when features are continuous, correlated, or when the task demands more than 85-90% accuracy.
Both support incremental learning via partial_fit, making them practical for streaming or online classification.
For maximum accuracy on text tasks, start with Naive Bayes as a fast baseline, then graduate to SVM or fine-tuned BERT.
: Counts how often each outcome occurs
: Multinomial exploits it, Bernoulli ignores it
: Multinomial has higher accuracy in practice
: Both are popular for text classification
Multinomial and Bernoulli Naive Bayes are the "text classification specialists" — Multinomial cares about
words appear (counts matter), making it better for long documents where repetition indicates importance, while Bernoulli only cares about

words appear (binary presence), making it faster and better for short texts where absence of a word is also informative, so choose Multinomial for word frequencies and long documents, and Bernoulli for binary features and short texts.
